page: Vultr voc-g-24c-96gb-480s-amd is an x86_64 architecture server within the Optimized Cloud Compute family. It features 24 shared vCPUs mapped to 12 physical AMD EPYC cores running at 2.0 GHz, supporting two threads per core. The system is equipped with 96.0 GB of system memory, providing a balanced 4.0 GB of RAM per core. Local storage consists of a bundled 480 GB NVMe SSD, and the instance includes one complimentary public IPv4 address.

Benchmark data indicates strong CPU capabilities, with stress-ng single-core performance ranking in the top tier at 3208.88, and multi-core performance in the top 25% at 74749.71. Passmark memory testing also places in the top 25% with a score of 2891.74. Memory bandwidth remains average, measuring 126.87 GB/sec for large block read-only operations and 61.71 GB/sec for large block write operations. In language model inference tests using a small 135M model, the server achieves a top-tier text generation speed of 342.31 tokens/sec, while medium 7B model text generation operates at an average speed of 25.54 tokens/sec.

The primary architectural tradeoff of this server is its shared CPU allocation, which may introduce resource contention under sustained multi-tenant workloads despite the high physical core count. However, the combination of a 1:4 vCPU-to-RAM ratio and fast NVMe storage offers solid resource density for general-purpose applications. This configuration is suitable for multi-threaded decompression tasks, static web serving, intermediate database operations, and small-scale language model inference where dedicated CPU performance is not strictly required.
description: The Vultr voc-g-24c-96gb-480s-amd is an x86_64 server in the Optimized Cloud Compute family, configured with 24 shared vCPUs across 12 physical AMD EPYC cores at 2.0 GHz. It features 96.0 GB of RAM, a 480 GB NVMe SSD, and one public IPv4 address. Benchmarks show top-tier single-core CPU performance and strong multi-core capabilities, alongside average memory bandwidth. While the shared CPU allocation presents a potential tradeoff for sustained workloads, the balanced 1:4 vCPU-to-RAM ratio provides cost-efficient resource density. This server is suited for static web serving, multi-threaded decompression, and moderate database operations.
